title: What an American thinks about Czeck Republic - Bike touring May 2010

transcript.text: Hello, this video is what I, an American thinks about Czech Republic.
I was there like four days, and, um, it's a cool place.
I came, I went from Vienna to Czech to Prague to up north to Poland, and, um, Czech Republic.
I didn't have to get stamped in. I looked around, I looked around, I saw the cop thing, but it was all closed, so I just went in.
It became very less, much less populated, but I guess it was less populated in the eastern part of Austria than the western part, and flatter.
So basically you can camp wherever you want, like, finding a place to camp in the bushes is no problem.
And it's actually a forest, and it's beautiful forest, I mean, it's like rainforests, you know, like, with all the rain, just trees,
And then you got the little canopy protecting all the little, little plants below, you know.
And it's been raining, like, hardcore every day for the last couple weeks, so I'm just gonna go for another week.
So it's like, I guess, Czech Republic, Poland, Austria, it's like rainforest, man.
But luckily it's late enough in the year that I'm not freezing my ass off, but I am getting wet as fuck.
So, I went into Prague.
Prague is beautiful.
You know, everybody's bragged about Prague, so I was, like, looking forward to going to Prague.
And it wasn't that expensive.
It was, like, 18, whatchamacallits, Prague dollars, whatever they're called.
And, um, which was, like, or I don't even remember, I don't know, 450 Prague dollars, which was, like, 20 bucks.
I don't even know.
I never looked it up, but I was there three nights.
Um, I was there for the marathon, and I took video of the marathon.
I took a lot of videos, actually, of the marathon and, um, of, like, polka music and stuff.
And even kids in the hostel and stuff.
It was really good, but then I accidentally, like, erased a whole bunch of videos.
So, I lost all of it.
So, anyways, I won't do that again.
I learned my lesson with that.
But, um, um, Prague is, like, it reminds, you know that movie Shrek?
Remember Never Never Land?
Where you have, like, a castle inside of the walls?
It's like that.
Like, more than any place I've ever seen.
Like, you have this castle, and then you have, like, the walls with the holes for the archers.
And then this huge ravine with a river going through it.
Um, so that would have been a great defendatory place.
Defensive place.
I'm a little drunk.
At least on the north side.
And then it's up on a hill towards the east where the river is.
And then the south.
And then towards the west.
It was kind of normal.
So, I guess they had to have bigger walls there.
But, anyways, it's really beautiful.
Their central church there.
There were so many tourists, though.
It's the most tourist place I've ever been in my entire life.
I mean, more than Rome.
But I was there for the marathon, too, you know.
But, um, people everywhere.
And it's in my video, you can see.
But, um, it's also the most pedestrian city I've ever been.
I mean, the whole center, it's all pedestrian.
It's just, like, a maze of only pedestrian streets, cobblestone.
And it's really beautiful that way.
And lots of, you know, like, plazas and stuff.
And so, I mean, yeah.
Prague is a great place to check out.
I saw lots of time massage signs everywhere.
Which was, like, I was, like, what?
I was, like, confused.
And then I looked at one.
And it's, like, real time massage.
Like, they had, like, four, um, ex-instructors of Wat Poe working at one place.
So, somebody, like, brought a whole bunch of Thais massage Thai people to Prague.
And I guess, and, so maybe I should have gotten a Thai massage.
I would have been getting a Thai massage at Wat Poe, which I actually never did.
I got some Thai massage from some, like, old man on Kalsan Road.
It totally sucked.
So, you know what?
In retrospect, I should have gotten a Thai massage there.
But, anyways, I didn't.
But, um, that was interesting.
And the Czech people, um, they're very friendly.
They speak less English than the Austrians, who speak a lot less than the Germans.
Um, and out, like, out in the outskirts, I think I only talked to, like, a couple guys who could speak English.
I mean, you pretty much don't speak English.
Inside the city, they do.
And if you kind of imply that they don't, it seems like they get maybe a little upset.
Because I asked some guy, he's, like, hello.
And I was, like, oh, yeah, you speak English?
He's, like, I said hello.
Doesn't that mean I speak English?
So, I was, like, okay.
And then this other guy, he's, like, I'm, like, yeah, I want the strongest earphones.
You know, strong?
And I flex my arm, like, strong.
And I was, like, I understand what strong means.
And I was, like, uh.
So, um, but, um, I mean, they speak way better English in Czech than they do in Poland.
But that's another video.
So, and then I went to the north, and they had lots of, um, lots of, like, fields of, uh, of this yellow flower.
And also fields of dandelions.
So, the, and, you know, lots of, like, little villages.
Like, tiny little villages.
Then they have the sign for the village.
And then, like, one minute later, you're out of the village.
Like, in America, they wouldn't, if a village that small, they would not have a sign for it.
Maybe they'd have a name for it or something, but the government wouldn't put up a sign and be, like, Billy Bob Joe and his family, you know.
But it's, like, an official village there.
And, you know, so then you go through a village.
Because I got lost, and I had to go through, like, half the day.
I had to go through, like, tiny little villages on tiny little roads and stuff.
But it was pretty.
Um, and they're really into Jesus.
They have Jesus Christ statues all over the place.
In Prague, like, the, or Czech Republic in the little, like, villages and stuff.
And, um, yeah.
I stayed at a campsite.
And in the morning, I was like, yeah, I stayed here.
And they were like, nah, blah, blah, blah.
I think what she meant was they hadn't opened for a campsite yet.
And so they weren't smiling at me.
I didn't know the people.
But they didn't charge me.
But, yeah, I mean, Czech Republic, I guess it's one of those Eastern Bloc countries that was communist just a few years back.
So they're kind of poor.
And I get, even the guy at the hostel was saying that they're, you know, poor there and they're not happy about the politicians.
But they, no, no, nobody participates in politics because they're, they feel like they can't do anything about it.
So they just don't participate, you know, or whatever.
And so they kind of, they're not as, like, cheery and happy as, like, in Austria and Germany and, like, first world countries, you know.
They're kind of more, like, gloomy, kind of like in Poland, you know.
Like, if you, if you look at them on the street and you're like, hey, they're like, huh.
You know, whereas in Germany they'd be like, hey, yay, you know.
But, um, but, I mean, they're nice and everything.
Um, but, um, but, yeah, it was cheaper.
Like, the outskirts, you know, food, I could tell was cheaper and the food was good.
Um, but they're also, like, more formal.
And also in Poland, but I'll talk about that on another video, the Poland video.
But, yeah, they're more formal.
Like, like, like a waiter, you know, like in America or, like, Germany or whatever would be like, hey, what's up, you know, what do you want?
But they're, they're, like, stand up straight, you know.
And then you, like, sit down and they're, like, they, they waltz over, you know.
And they're just very, and when they place the, you know, the forks and stuff, they put it, like, perfectly, you know.
They're just more formal.
So, but, um, Czech Republic, that's my experience with Czech.
I'm going to head into the Slovakia later.
I'm in Poland now, but I'm not going to do a Poland video because, um, I'm still in Poland.
And I don't like doing videos until after I'm completely out of the country because something wacky could happen before I leave.
So, anyways, that's what an American thinks about Czech Republic.
Prague's really beautiful, though.
You want to check it out.
